title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, enrich, and explore the General Conference talk dataset. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from the raw Church website pages to the final CSV and downstream exploration.

title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, enrich, and explore the General Conference talk dataset. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from the raw Church website pages to the final CSV and downstream exploration.

title: Technical Report
text:
1. Data Extraction
The collection process is implemented in the src/gc_analysis/data.py module. The main entry point is data_pipeline(), which loops over the requested years and conference months and calls grab_gc_talk() for each pair.
The extraction step follows this sequence:

Request the General Conference index page for a specific year and month.
Parse the HTML with BeautifulSoup.
Locate each talk link on the conference page.
Visit each talk page individually.
Extract the title, speaker, role, kicker, and body text.

The collected fields are stored in a dictionary first and then converted into a pandas DataFrame. That approach keeps the extraction logic simple and makes the output easy to inspect before later processing.

title: Technical Report
text:
2. Data Structure
The raw dataset produced by the pipeline contains these columns:

title
author
role
kicker
text
year
month

After embeddings are generated, an additional embedding column is added.
This structure is important because it separates what was scraped from what was derived later. Anyone rerunning the pipeline can compare the raw text fields against cleaned or embedded versions without guessing how the data changed.

title: Technical Report
text:
3. Cleaning And Standardization
Once the talks are collected, clean_data() standardizes the role field. The source pages use full role names, but the analysis uses compact labels such as A for Apostle and RS for Relief Society.
This step reduces variation in the dataset and makes grouping, counting, and plotting more reliable. It also keeps role-based summaries readable when exploring the collection.

title: Technical Report
text:
4. Embedding Generation
The next step is semantic enrichment. generate_embeddings() sends each non-null talk text value to Ollama using the embeddinggemma model and stores the returned vector as a JSON string.
Why this matters:

It makes similarity analysis possible.
It supports clustering and search-style exploration.
It preserves the vectors in CSV form so the dataset can be reloaded without recomputing everything immediately.

The embeddings are saved as text for compatibility with CSV storage, and load_embeddings_from_csv() later converts them back into numeric arrays.

title: Technical Report
text:
5. Persisting The Output
When save_csv=True, the pipeline writes the final dataset to data/gc_talks.csv. That file becomes the reproducible artifact for the rest of the project.
To recreate the dataset, rerun the pipeline with the same years and months arguments. The defaults collect April and October 2025, but the inputs can be changed to reproduce the exact slice used in any analysis.
Example:
from gc_analysis.data import data_pipeline

df = data_pipeline(years=[2025], months=["04", "10"], save_csv=True, verbose=True)

title: Technical Report
text:
6. Exploration Workflow
After the CSV is created, the dataset can be explored with pandas or any downstream notebook or app. A typical exploration sequence is:

Load the CSV with load_embeddings_from_csv().
Check dataset size, unique speakers, and role counts.
Review talk titles, summaries, and speakers for quality control.
Filter by year, month, author, or role.
Use embeddings for similarity search or broader thematic exploration.

Example exploration code:
from gc_analysis.data import load_embeddings_from_csv

df = load_embeddings_from_csv("data/gc_talks.csv")

print(len(df))
print(df["role"].value_counts())
print(df[["title", "author", "role"]].head())

title: Technical Report
text:
7. Reproducibility Notes
To reproduce the analysis reliably, the following assumptions should be kept consistent:

The same source site structure is available on the Church website.
Ollama is installed and running locally.
The embeddinggemma model is available.
The same year and month inputs are used when calling the pipeline.

If any of those inputs change, the resulting dataset may differ. The pipeline is intentionally explicit about these dependencies so future runs can be traced back to the original extraction and enrichment steps.

title: Technical Report
text:
8. Summary
The project workflow is straightforward: scrape the talks, standardize the metadata, generate embeddings, save the dataset, and then explore the result from a reproducible CSV. This page is the narrative record of that process.

title: Getting Started
text:
First, ensure you have the required dependencies installed. The package requires: - requests - for web scraping - beautifulsoup4 - for HTML parsing - pandas - for data manipulation - ollama - for generating embeddings - numpy - for numerical operations
Install them with:
pip install requests beautifulsoup4 pandas ollama numpy

title: Getting Started
text:
First, ensure you have the required dependencies installed. The package requires: - requests - for web scraping - beautifulsoup4 - for HTML parsing - pandas - for data manipulation - ollama - for generating embeddings - numpy - for numerical operations
Install them with:
pip install requests beautifulsoup4 pandas ollama numpy

title: Getting Started
text:
Quick Start

1. Basic Data Collection
The simplest way to get started is to use the data_pipeline() function to collect talks from recent General Conferences:
from gc_analysis.data import data_pipeline

# Collect talks from April and October 2025
df = data_pipeline(years=[2025], months=["04", "10"])

# View the first few talks
print(df.head())
This will: 1. Scrape all talks from the specified conferences 2. Extract speaker information and talk content 3. Clean and standardize the data 4. Generate embeddings for each talk 5. Save everything to data/gc_talks.csv


2. Access Your Data
After running the pipeline, you can work with the collected data:
from gc_analysis.data import load_embeddings_from_csv

# Load the saved data with embeddings
df = load_embeddings_from_csv("data/gc_talks.csv")

# Explore the data
print(f"Total talks: {len(df)}")
print(f"Unique speakers: {df['author'].nunique()}")
print(f"Speakers by role:\n{df['role'].value_counts()}")


3. Analyze the Data
Now you can perform various analyses on the collected talks:
# Find talks by a specific speaker
speaker_talks = df[df['author'] == 'Nelson Russell M.']
print(f"Talks by Nelson Russell M.: {len(speaker_talks)}")

# Find talks by a specific role
apostle_talks = df[df['role'] == 'A']
print(f"Total talks by Apostles: {len(apostle_talks)}")

# View talk information
for idx, row in df.head(3).iterrows():
    print(f"\nTitle: {row['title']}")
    print(f"Speaker: {row['author']} ({row['role']})")
    print(f"Summary: {row['kicker']}")


4. Collect Multiple Years
You can easily expand your dataset to include multiple years:
# Collect talks from April and October of 2024 and 2025
df = data_pipeline(years=[2024, 2025], months=["04", "10"], save_csv=True)

# This will take longer due to increased number of talks

title: Getting Started
text:
Common Patterns

Silencing Progress Output
If you want to run the pipeline without verbose output:
df = data_pipeline(verbose=False)


Collecting Without Saving CSV
If you only want the data in memory:
df = data_pipeline(save_csv=False)
# Data is not saved to CSV, only returned


Combining Multiple Collections
You can run the pipeline multiple times and combine results:
import pandas as pd

# First collection
df1 = data_pipeline(years=[2024], months=["04"], verbose=False)

# Second collection
df2 = data_pipeline(years=[2024], months=["10"], verbose=False)

# Combine them
df_combined = pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)
print(f"Total talks collected: {len(df_combined)}")

title: Getting Started
text:
Next Steps

Check out the Documentation for detailed function documentation
Explore the data interactively in a Jupyter notebook
Use embeddings for similarity analysis and clustering
Build visualizations of speaker frequency, role distributions, and more

title: Getting Started
text:
Troubleshooting
Issue: “ollama” module not found - Solution: Make sure Ollama is installed and running. Install with pip install ollama and run the Ollama service.
Issue: Web scraping is very slow or times out - Solution: This is normal for collecting many talks. The script includes progress bars using tqdm to track progress. Large collections may take 10-30 minutes.
Issue: Embeddings are not being generated - Solution: Ensure Ollama is running and the embeddinggemma model is available. You may need to pull the model with ollama pull embeddinggemma.

title: Documentation
text: The gc_analysis package provides a suite of functions for scraping, processing, and analyzing General Conference talks. This page documents the main functions available in the package.

title: Documentation
text: The gc_analysis package provides a suite of functions for scraping, processing, and analyzing General Conference talks. This page documents the main functions available in the package.

title: Documentation
text:
Main Functions

data_pipeline()
The primary function for orchestrating the entire data collection and processing workflow.
Signature:
data_pipeline(years=[2025], months=["04", "10"], save_csv=True, verbose=True)
Parameters:

years (list, default: [2025]): List of conference years to collect (e.g., [2024, 2025])
months (list, default: ["04", "10"]): List of conference months as strings (e.g., ["04", "10"] for April and October)
save_csv (bool, default: True): Whether to save the processed data to data/gc_talks.csv
verbose (bool, default: True): Whether to print progress messages during execution

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: A pandas DataFrame containing the collected and processed talks with the following columns:

title: Talk title
author: Speaker name
role: Speaker’s position/responsibility (cleaned)
kicker: Summary/description of the talk
text: Full talk text
year: Conference year
month: Conference month
embedding: Vector embedding of the talk text (as a JSON string in CSV)


Example:
from gc_analysis.data import data_pipeline

# Collect talks from April and October 2025
df = data_pipeline(years=[2025], months=["04", "10"])



grab_gc_talk()
Scrapes talks from the Church website for a specified year and month.
Signature:
grab_gc_talk(data_dict, year, month)
Parameters:

data_dict (dict): Dictionary to accumulate talk data with keys: title, author, role, kicker, text, year, month
year (int): Conference year (e.g., 2025)
month (str): Conference month as a string (e.g., "04" for April)

Returns:

dict: Updated data_dict with new talks appended

Note: This function is typically called by data_pipeline() and rarely needs to be called directly.



extract_data()
Helper function to extract text content from HTML elements using BeautifulSoup.
Signature:
extract_data(soup, tag, class_name)
Parameters:

soup (BeautifulSoup): Parsed HTML document
tag (str): HTML tag name (e.g., 'h1', 'p', 'div')
class_name (str or None): CSS class name to filter by, or None to search by tag only

Returns:

str or None: Text content if the element is found, otherwise None

Example:
import requests
import bs4
from gc_analysis.data import extract_data

response = requests.get("https://example.com")
soup = bs4.BeautifulSoup(response.content, "html.parser")
title = extract_data(soup, 'h1', None)



clean_data()
Standardizes speaker roles across the dataset by mapping full role names to abbreviations.
Signature:
clean_data(df)
Parameters:

df (pd.DataFrame): DataFrame with a role column containing speaker positions

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: DataFrame with cleaned role values

Role Mappings:



Full Name
Abbreviation




Relief Society
RS


First Presidency
FP


Apostle
A


Young Women
YW


Young Men
YM


Primary
P


Sunday School
SS


Seventy
70


Bishop
B


President of the Church
Prophet


Managing Director
MD


Ward
Member



Example:
from gc_analysis.data import clean_data
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({'role': ['Apostle', 'Relief Society', 'Young Men']})
df_cleaned = clean_data(df)
# df_cleaned['role'] now contains: ['A', 'RS', 'YM']



generate_embeddings()
Generates vector embeddings for talk text using Ollama’s embedding model.
Signature:
generate_embeddings(df)
Parameters:

df (pd.DataFrame): DataFrame with a text column containing talk content

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: DataFrame with a new embedding column containing embeddings as JSON strings

Notes:

Requires Ollama to be installed and running
Uses the embeddinggemma model for generating embeddings
Only generates embeddings for non-null text entries
Embeddings are stored as JSON strings for CSV compatibility




load_embeddings_from_csv()
Loads a CSV file and converts JSON string embeddings back to numpy arrays.
Signature:
load_embeddings_from_csv(csv_path)
Parameters:

csv_path (str): Path to the CSV file containing the talk data

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: DataFrame with embeddings converted to numpy arrays; rows with invalid embeddings are removed

Example:
from gc_analysis.data import load_embeddings_from_csv

df = load_embeddings_from_csv("data/gc_talks.csv")
# Use embeddings for similarity analysis

title: General Conference Analysis
text:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

import phate
import plotly.express as px


from gc_analysis.data import data_pipeline, load_embeddings_from_csv

try:
    # Load existing CSV and auto-parse embeddings from JSON strings
    df = load_embeddings_from_csv("./data/gc_talks.csv")
    print(f"Loaded {len(df)} talks with embeddings from CSV")
except FileNotFoundError:
    # Generate new data if CSV doesn't exist
    print("CSV not found, generating new data...")
    df = data_pipeline(years=list(range(2018, 2026)), months=["04", "10"])
    # Reload to ensure embeddings are properly converted
    df = load_embeddings_from_csv("./data/gc_talks.csv")

Loaded 1972 talks with embeddings from CSV



subset_df = df[df['text'].notnull()]

phate_op = phate.PHATE(n_components=2)                    # Create PHATE reducer for 2D output
embeddings = np.stack(subset_df['embedding'].values)             # Stack all embeddings into a matrix
phate_result = phate_op.fit_transform(embeddings)         # Apply PHATE to get 2D coordinates

# Step 2: Create an interactive scatter plot
fig = px.scatter(
    x=phate_result[:, 0],                                 # X-axis: first PHATE dimension
    y=phate_result[:, 1],                                 # Y-axis: second PHATE dimension
    color=subset_df["role"],                                     # Color points by conference year
    hover_name=subset_df["title"],                               # Show talk title on hover
    hover_data={                                          # Additional info to show on hover
        "author": subset_df["author"],
        "role": subset_df["role"],
        "year": subset_df["year"],
        "month": subset_df["month"]
    },
)

# Display the interactive visualization
fig.show()

title: General Conference Analysis
text:
This package provides tools for exploring and scraping talks from the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints General Conferences. It automates the collection of conference talk data, including speaker information, talk content, and AI-generated embeddings for analysis.
Test this out with our streamlit app here!

title: General Conference Analysis
text:
This package provides tools for exploring and scraping talks from the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints General Conferences. It automates the collection of conference talk data, including speaker information, talk content, and AI-generated embeddings for analysis.
Test this out with our streamlit app here!

title: General Conference Analysis
text:
Key Features

Web Scraping: Automatically downloads talks from the official church website for specified conferences and years
Data Extraction: Extracts structured information including:

Talk title and full text
Speaker name and role
Talk summary (kicker text)
Conference date

Data Cleaning: Standardizes speaker roles across the dataset (e.g., “Apostle” → “A”, “Relief Society” → “RS”)
Embeddings Generation: Creates AI-powered embeddings of talk content using Ollama for similarity analysis and exploration
CSV Export: Saves processed data to a structured CSV file for easy analysis

title: General Conference Analysis
text:
Main Pipeline
The data_pipeline() function in src/gc_analysis/data.py orchestrates the entire workflow:

Scrapes talks for specified years and conference months
Extracts relevant metadata and content from each talk
Cleans and standardizes the data
Generates vector embeddings for the talk text
Exports results to CSV format

You can customize the pipeline by specifying years, months, and output preferences.
